agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Design a unified API Gateway architecture for a logistics platform serving three distinct consumer types (mobile, B2B partner, internal). The gateway centralizes authentication, rate limiting, and traffic isolation—replacing the current inconsistent per-service implementations. The architecture uses a single gateway deployment with three logical ingress lanes, each applying consumer-specific policies, fronting a shared backend service mesh isolated via network segmentation.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"API Gateway (Edge)","responsibility":"Single entry point for all external traffic. Terminates TLS, routes requests, enforces authentication, applies rate limiting, transforms headers, and emits telemetry. Runs two listener groups: one for mobile (public), one for B2B partners (public with IP allowlisting option).","boundaries":"Owns cross-cutting concerns only: auth, rate limiting, routing, request/response transformation, logging. Zero business logic. Never accesses databases directly."},{"name":"Internal Gateway (Mesh)","responsibility":"Lightweight internal gateway or service mesh sidecar for internal service-to-service traffic. No authentication but enforces mTLS for identity, circuit breaking, and observability. Physically separated from external gateway.","boundaries":"Only reachable from internal network. No external ingress. Enforces network policies that reject traffic originating from external gateway's subnet."},{"name":"Auth Service","responsibility":"Issues and validates JWT tokens for mobile users (OAuth 2.0 + PKCE). Validates API keys for B2B partners and resolves them to partner identity with associated rate-limit tier and permissions. Exposes a fast /validate endpoint consumed by the gateway.","boundaries":"Owns identity and credential storage. Does not make authorization decisions beyond identity resolution—authorization is handled by downstream services using claims in the token/header."},{"name":"Rate Limiter","responsibility":"Distributed rate limiting using a sliding window algorithm backed by Redis. Applies per-consumer-type policies: 100 req/min per user (mobile), 1,000 req/min per partner (B2B). Returns rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Remaining, Retry-After).","boundaries":"Stateless compute, stateful counters in Redis. Gateway calls it as an inline filter—not a separate network hop if deployed as a gateway plugin."},{"name":"Webhook Dispatcher","responsibility":"Reliable delivery of event notifications to B2B partners. Accepts domain events from internal services, persists them to an outbox table, and delivers with exponential backoff retry (up to 5 attempts). Partners register endpoints via self-service portal.","boundaries":"Owns webhook subscriptions and delivery state. Does not interpret event payloads—passes them through. Decoupled from the gateway; triggered by domain events, not HTTP requests."},{"name":"Backend Services","responsibility":"Core logistics domain services (order management, tracking, routing, inventory, etc.). Contain all business logic. After gateway migration, these services shed all auth and rate-limiting code.","boundaries":"Accept requests only from the API Gateway (external) or Internal Gateway (internal). Trust the gateway-injected headers (X-Consumer-Type, X-Consumer-Id, X-Partner-Id) for identity. Validate header presence but do not re-authenticate."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Mobile App","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS with Bearer JWT token in Authorization header. Gateway validates JWT signature and expiry via Auth Service, then injects X-Consumer-Type: mobile and X-Consumer-Id: <user_id> headers.","coupling_type":"loose — standard OAuth 2.0 / JWT, client only knows public API surface"},{"from":"Partner Integration","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS with API key in X-API-Key header. Gateway resolves key via Auth Service, injects X-Consumer-Type: partner and X-Partner-Id: <partner_id>. Versioned API (v1, v2) via URL path prefix.","coupling_type":"loose — API key is opaque credential, versioned contract"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Auth Service","contract":"Internal gRPC or HTTP call to POST /validate with token or API key. Returns consumer identity, type, and rate-limit tier. Response cached at gateway for 30s (JWT) or 5min (API key) to reduce latency.","coupling_type":"tight (runtime dependency) — mitigated by caching and circuit breaker"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Rate Limiter","contract":"Inline plugin call (not network hop if co-located). Input: consumer_id + consumer_type. Output: allow/deny + remaining quota. On Rate Limiter failure, fail-open with degraded logging.","coupling_type":"tight (inline) — fail-open design prevents cascading failure"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Backend Services","contract":"HTTP/gRPC with gateway-injected identity headers. Backend services validate header presence via middleware but trust gateway for authentication.","coupling_type":"moderate — services depend on header contract, not on gateway implementation"},{"from":"Internal Services","to":"Internal Gateway (Mesh)","contract":"mTLS with service identity certificates. No user-level auth. Routing via service discovery (DNS or registry).","coupling_type":"loose — standard service mesh pattern"},{"from":"Backend Services","to":"Webhook Dispatcher","contract":"Domain events published to message broker (e.g., Kafka/SQS). Webhook Dispatcher subscribes to relevant topics and delivers to partner endpoints.","coupling_type":"loose — async event-driven, no direct coupling"}],"data_flow":"Mobile/Partner → DNS/LB → API Gateway (TLS termination) → Auth filter (validate identity, cache result) → Rate Limit filter (check/decrement quota) → Route to Backend Service → Backend processes request → Response back through gateway (strips internal headers) → Client. For webhooks: Backend Service → Domain Event → Message Broker → Webhook Dispatcher → Partner Endpoint. Internal services bypass the edge gateway entirely, communicating through the Internal Gateway/mesh with mTLS.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Gateway Offloading","rationale":"Centralizes cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, logging) that are currently inconsistently implemented across services. Single place to enforce policy eliminates drift."},{"pattern":"Backends for Frontends (BFF) variant — Consumer Lanes","rationale":"Rather than three separate gateways (expensive to operate), use one gateway with three route groups that apply different filter chains. Mobile lane: JWT + 100rpm. Partner lane: API key + 1000rpm + webhook registration. Internal lane: separate network path entirely."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig (Migration)","rationale":"Migrate services behind the gateway incrementally. Each service is moved by updating DNS/routing. Old direct-access paths are deprecated with a sunset header, then removed."},{"pattern":"Sidecar / Service Mesh for Internal Traffic","rationale":"Internal services need isolation from external traffic, not a heavyweight gateway. A mesh sidecar (Envoy/Linkerd) or simple internal ALB provides mTLS, observability, and circuit breaking without the auth overhead."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox for Webhooks","rationale":"Guarantees at-least-once delivery to partners. Events are written to an outbox table in the same transaction as the domain state change, then delivered asynchronously. Prevents lost webhooks on service crashes."},{"pattern":"Fail-Open Rate Limiting","rationale":"If Redis is unavailable, the gateway allows requests through rather than rejecting all traffic. Counters recover when Redis returns. Protects availability at the cost of temporary rate-limit bypass."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Identity & Access","classification":"generic","aggregates":["UserCredential","PartnerApiKey","Session"]},{"name":"Traffic Policy","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["RateLimitPolicy","ConsumerTier","RouteConfiguration"]},{"name":"Webhook Delivery","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["WebhookSubscription","DeliveryAttempt","OutboxEvent"]},{"name":"Logistics Core","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order","Shipment","Route","Inventory"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Identity & Access","downstream":"API Gateway (Edge)","relationship":"Open Host Service — Auth Service exposes a published validation API consumed by the gateway. Gateway is a conformist to the identity model."},{"upstream":"Traffic Policy","downstream":"API Gateway (Edge)","relationship":"Shared Kernel — rate limit policies and route configs are shared between the gateway config and the policy management system."},{"upstream":"Logistics Core","downstream":"Webhook Delivery","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Core services publish domain events; Webhook Delivery subscribes. Core team (upstream) defines event schemas; Webhook team conforms."},{"upstream":"API Gateway (Edge)","downstream":"Logistics Core","relationship":"Anti-Corruption Layer — Backend services have a thin middleware ACL that translates gateway headers into domain-level caller context, preventing gateway implementation details from leaking into domain code."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single gateway with consumer lanes vs. separate gateways per consumer type","gains":"Lower operational burden — one deployment, one config pipeline, one monitoring stack. Shared infrastructure cost.","sacrifices":"Blast radius is wider — a gateway misconfiguration can affect all consumer types. More complex routing configuration.","alternatives_considered":["Separate gateway per consumer type (higher isolation, 3x operational cost)","No gateway — fix auth consistency at service level (doesn't solve rate limiting centralization, high ongoing maintenance)"]},{"decision":"Fail-open rate limiting","gains":"Availability preserved during Redis outages. Users experience no disruption.","sacrifices":"Temporary loss of rate-limit enforcement during outages. A determined attacker could exploit this window.","alternatives_considered":["Fail-closed (reject all on Redis failure — too disruptive for a logistics platform where uptime is critical)","Local in-memory rate limiting fallback (approximate but better than nothing — good Phase 2 enhancement)"]},{"decision":"Gateway validates auth but services make authorization decisions","gains":"Separation of concerns: gateway handles 'who are you', services handle 'are you allowed to do this'. Services retain control over their authorization rules.","sacrifices":"Authorization logic remains distributed across services (but this is correct — authorization is domain-specific).","alternatives_considered":["Full auth + authz at gateway (couples business rules to infrastructure, creates bottleneck for policy changes)","OPA sidecar for authorization (good for uniform policies, overkill when authz rules are deeply domain-specific)"]},{"decision":"Transactional outbox for webhooks instead of direct dispatch","gains":"At-least-once delivery guarantee. No lost events on crashes. Retry with backoff built in.","sacrifices":"Higher latency for webhook delivery (seconds vs milliseconds). Requires outbox table and poller/CDC process.","alternatives_considered":["Direct HTTP dispatch from service (simpler but unreliable — lost events on failure)","CDC from main database (elegant but complex infrastructure dependency on Debezium/similar)"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Gateway technology choice","evolution_path":"Architecture is gateway-product-agnostic. Can start with Kong/AWS API Gateway/Envoy and swap later. The consumer-lane pattern works on any gateway that supports route-level filter chains."},{"area":"Auth mechanism evolution","evolution_path":"Auth Service is a separate component. Can migrate from API keys to OAuth client credentials for partners, or add OIDC federation, without touching the gateway or backend services."},{"area":"Rate limiting algorithm","evolution_path":"Sliding window, token bucket, or leaky bucket can be swapped by changing the Rate Limiter plugin. Consumer-facing contract (HTTP rate limit headers) stays stable."},{"area":"Webhook delivery mechanism","evolution_path":"Can evolve from simple outbox polling to CDC-based streaming. Can add fan-out, filtering, and transformation without changing the event publishing contract."},{"area":"Internal mesh evolution","evolution_path":"Can start with simple network segmentation + internal ALB, then evolve to full service mesh (Istio/Linkerd) when operational maturity warrants it."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Gateway-injected header contract","reason":"All backend services will depend on X-Consumer-Type, X-Consumer-Id, X-Partner-Id headers. Changing this contract requires coordinated updates across all services. Define it carefully upfront and version it."},{"area":"Rate limit tier definitions","reason":"Partner contracts may encode specific rate limits into SLAs. Changing tier structures has business/legal implications beyond engineering."},{"area":"External API versioning scheme","reason":"Once partners integrate against /v1/*, the versioning strategy (URL path prefix) is locked in. Cannot easily switch to header-based versioning later."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Gateway becomes single point of failure for all external traffic","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy gateway in multi-AZ active-active configuration. Health checks with automatic failover. Canary deployments for config changes. Synthetic monitoring of all three consumer lanes."},{"risk":"Auth Service latency adds to every request","severity":"high","mitigation":"Aggressive caching at gateway: 30s for JWT validation results, 5min for API key resolution. Circuit breaker with cached-result fallback. Auth Service itself should have <5ms p99 (in-memory key validation)."},{"risk":"Migration period: some services behind gateway, some not — inconsistent auth enforcement","severity":"high","mitigation":"Strangler fig migration with strict ordering: highest-risk services first. During migration, old direct paths enforce auth via existing (inconsistent) implementations — no regression. Set hard deadline for full migration."},{"risk":"Redis failure causes temporary rate-limit bypass","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Fail-open with alerting. Add local in-memory approximate rate limiter as fallback (Phase 2). Monitor for abuse patterns during Redis outages."},{"risk":"Webhook delivery backpressure from slow partner endpoints","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Per-partner delivery queues with independent retry. Circuit breaker per partner endpoint. Dead-letter queue after max retries with partner notification."},{"risk":"Internal services accidentally exposed through external gateway","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Network-level isolation: internal services are in a separate VPC/subnet with no route from external gateway. Defense in depth: gateway route config explicitly allowlists only external-facing service paths. Regular audit of exposed routes."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Weeks 1-3): Deploy gateway infrastructure with mobile consumer lane. Migrate 1-2 non-critical services behind gateway. Validate auth offloading and rate limiting. Phase 2 (Weeks 4-6): Add B2B partner lane with API key auth and rate limiting. Migrate remaining external-facing services. Phase 3 (Weeks 7-8): Deploy webhook dispatcher. Migrate partner webhook integrations. Phase 4 (Weeks 9-10): Establish internal gateway/mesh. Remove legacy auth code from backend services. Sunset direct-access paths.","files_to_create_or_modify":["infrastructure/api-gateway/gateway-config.yaml — Gateway route definitions, filter chains per consumer lane","infrastructure/api-gateway/rate-limit-policies.yaml — Per-tier rate limit configurations","services/auth-service/src/ — Auth validation service (if not existing)","services/webhook-dispatcher/src/ — Outbox-based webhook delivery service","shared/middleware/gateway-headers.ts — Backend middleware to parse and validate gateway-injected headers (thin ACL)","infrastructure/terraform/gateway.tf — Infrastructure as code for gateway deployment","docs/adr/001-api-gateway-architecture.md — Architecture decision record"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use declarative gateway configuration (YAML/HCL, not imperative code). Rate limit policies should be data-driven (configurable per consumer tier without code changes). Backend services should have a shared middleware library for gateway header parsing — do not let each team implement their own. Webhook dispatcher should use the transactional outbox pattern with idempotency keys to prevent duplicate delivery.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put business logic in the gateway — it must remain a pure infrastructure concern. The moment domain rules leak into gateway config, you've created an unmaintainable coupling.","Do NOT skip the strangler fig approach and attempt a big-bang migration. That's how you get outages.","Do NOT use the gateway for internal service-to-service calls. Internal traffic must have a separate path — mixing it creates a security boundary violation and a performance bottleneck.","Do NOT implement rate limiting per-service anymore. All rate limiting must go through the gateway to prevent the current inconsistency problem from recurring.","Do NOT hardcode partner rate limits in gateway config. Store them in a database/config service and have the gateway fetch tier definitions. Partners will negotiate different limits."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Three-lane gateway pattern is well-established for multi-consumer platforms","Strangler fig migration is the safe path given existing inconsistent auth","Transactional outbox for webhooks is the industry-standard reliable delivery pattern","Network isolation for internal services is non-negotiable and straightforward"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific gateway product selection (depends on team's existing infrastructure: AWS-native vs Kubernetes-native vs self-managed)","Exact caching TTLs for auth validation (30s/5min are starting points — need load testing to tune)","Whether internal traffic needs a full service mesh or a simpler internal load balancer (depends on scale and operational maturity)"],"assumptions":["The platform runs on cloud infrastructure with VPC/network segmentation capabilities","Redis or equivalent is available or can be provisioned for distributed rate limiting","The team has operational capacity to manage a gateway deployment","Services currently communicate over HTTP/gRPC (not proprietary protocols)","Partner count is in the tens-to-hundreds range (not millions — that would change the API key resolution architecture)"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Design a unified API Gateway architecture for a logistics platform serving three distinct consumer types (mobile, B2B partner, internal). The gateway centralizes authentication, rate limiting, and traffic isolation—replacing the current inconsistent per-service implementations. The architecture uses a single gateway deployment with three logical ingress lanes, each applying consumer-specific policies, fronting a shared backend service mesh isolated via network segmentation.

content.thinking: Design an API gateway architecture for a logistics platform with three consumer types.

Rate limit policies should be data-driven (configurable per consumer tier without code changes). Backend services should have a shared middleware library for gateway header parsing — do not let each team implement their own. Webhook dispatcher should use the transactional outbox pattern with idempotency keys to prevent duplicate delivery.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put business logic in the gateway — it must remain a pure infrastructure concern. The moment domain rules leak into gateway config, you've created an unmaintainable coupling.","Do NOT skip the strangler fig approach and attempt a big-bang migration. That's how you get outages.","Do NOT use the gateway for internal service-to-service calls. Internal traffic must have a separate path — mixing it creates a security boundary violation and a performance bottleneck.","Do NOT implement rate limiting per-service anymore. All rate limiting must go through the gateway to prevent the current inconsistency problem from recurring.","Do NOT hardcode partner rate limits in gateway config. Store them in a database/config service and have the gateway fetch tier definitions. Partners will negotiate different limits."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Three-lane gateway pattern is well-established for multi-consumer platforms","Strangler fig migration is the safe path given existing inconsistent auth","Transactional outbox for webhooks is the industry-standard reliable delivery pattern","Network isolation for internal services is non-negotiable and straightforward"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific gateway product selection (depends on team's existing infrastructure: AWS-native vs Kubernetes-native vs self-managed)","Exact caching TTLs for auth validation (30s/5min are starting points — need load testing to tune)","Whether internal traffic needs a full service mesh or a simpler internal load balancer (depends on scale and operational maturity)"],"assumptions":["The platform runs on cloud infrastructure with VPC/network segmentation capabilities","Redis or equivalent is available or can be provisioned for distributed rate limiting","The team has operational capacity to manage a gateway deployment","Services currently communicate over HTTP/gRPC (not proprietary protocols)","Partner count is in the tens-to-hundreds range (not millions — that would change the API key resolution architecture)"]}}

trace.result: {"executive_summary":"Design a unified API Gateway architecture for a logistics platform serving three distinct consumer types (mobile, B2B partner, internal). The gateway centralizes authentication, rate limiting, and traffic isolation—replacing the current inconsistent per-service implementations. The architecture uses a single gateway deployment with three logical ingress lanes, each applying consumer-specific policies, fronting a shared backend service mesh isolated via network segmentation.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"API Gateway (Edge)","responsibility":"Single entry point for all external traffic. Terminates TLS, routes requests, enforces authentication, applies rate limiting, transforms headers, and emits telemetry. Runs two listener groups: one for mobile (public), one for B2B partners (public with IP allowlisting option).","boundaries":"Owns cross-cutting concerns only: auth, rate limiting, routing, request/response transformation, logging. Zero business logic. Never accesses databases directly."},{"name":"Internal Gateway (Mesh)","responsibility":"Lightweight internal gateway or service mesh sidecar for internal service-to-service traffic. No authentication but enforces mTLS for identity, circuit breaking, and observability. Physically separated from external gateway.","boundaries":"Only reachable from internal network. No external ingress. Enforces network policies that reject traffic originating from external gateway's subnet."},{"name":"Auth Service","responsibility":"Issues and validates JWT tokens for mobile users (OAuth 2.0 + PKCE). Validates API keys for B2B partners and resolves them to partner identity with associated rate-limit tier and permissions. Exposes a fast /validate endpoint consumed by the gateway.","boundaries":"Owns identity and credential storage. Does not make authorization decisions beyond identity resolution—authorization is handled by downstream services using claims in the token/header."},{"name":"Rate Limiter","responsibility":"Distributed rate limiting using a sliding window algorithm backed by Redis. Applies per-consumer-type policies: 100 req/min per user (mobile), 1,000 req/min per partner (B2B). Returns rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Remaining, Retry-After).","boundaries":"Stateless compute, stateful counters in Redis. Gateway calls it as an inline filter—not a separate network hop if deployed as a gateway plugin."},{"name":"Webhook Dispatcher","responsibility":"Reliable delivery of event notifications to B2B partners. Accepts domain events from internal services, persists them to an outbox table, and delivers with exponential backoff retry (up to 5 attempts). Partners register endpoints via self-service portal.","boundaries":"Owns webhook subscriptions and delivery state. Does not interpret event payloads—passes them through. Decoupled from the gateway; triggered by domain events, not HTTP requests."},{"name":"Backend Services","responsibility":"Core logistics domain services (order management, tracking, routing, inventory, etc.). Contain all business logic. After gateway migration, these services shed all auth and rate-limiting code.","boundaries":"Accept requests only from the API Gateway (external) or Internal Gateway (internal). Trust the gateway-injected headers (X-Consumer-Type, X-Consumer-Id, X-Partner-Id) for identity. Validate header presence but do not re-authenticate."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Mobile App","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS with Bearer JWT token in Authorization header. Gateway validates JWT signature and expiry via Auth Service, then injects X-Consumer-Type: mobile and X-Consumer-Id: <user_id> headers.","coupling_type":"loose — standard OAuth 2.0 / JWT, client only knows public API surface"},{"from":"Partner Integration","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS with API key in X-API-Key header. Gateway resolves key via Auth Service, injects X-Consumer-Type: partner and X-Partner-Id: <partner_id>. Versioned API (v1, v2) via URL path prefix.","coupling_type":"loose — API key is opaque credential, versioned contract"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Auth Service","contract":"Internal gRPC or HTTP call to POST /validate with token or API key. Returns consumer identity, type, and rate-limit tier. Response cached at gateway for 30s (JWT) or 5min (API key) to reduce latency.","coupling_type":"tight (runtime dependency) — mitigated by caching and circuit breaker"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Rate Limiter","contract":"Inline plugin call (not network hop if co-located). Input: consumer_id + consumer_type. Output: allow/deny + remaining quota. On Rate Limiter failure, fail-open with degraded logging.","coupling_type":"tight (inline) — fail-open design prevents cascading failure"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Backend Services","contract":"HTTP/gRPC with gateway-injected identity headers. Backend services validate header presence via middleware but trust gateway for authentication.","coupling_type":"moderate — services depend on header contract, not on gateway implementation"},{"from":"Internal Services","to":"Internal Gateway (Mesh)","contract":"mTLS with service identity certificates. No user-level auth. Routing via service discovery (DNS or registry).","coupling_type":"loose — standard service mesh pattern"},{"from":"Backend Services","to":"Webhook Dispatcher","contract":"Domain events published to message broker (e.g., Kafka/SQS). Webhook Dispatcher subscribes to relevant topics and delivers to partner endpoints.","coupling_type":"loose — async event-driven, no direct coupling"}],"data_flow":"Mobile/Partner → DNS/LB → API Gateway (TLS termination) → Auth filter (validate identity, cache result) → Rate Limit filter (check/decrement quota) → Route to Backend Service → Backend processes request → Response back through gateway (strips internal headers) → Client. For webhooks: Backend Service → Domain Event → Message Broker → Webhook Dispatcher → Partner Endpoint. Internal services bypass the edge gateway entirely, communicating through the Internal Gateway/mesh with mTLS.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Gateway Offloading","rationale":"Centralizes cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, logging) that are currently inconsistently implemented across services. Single place to enforce policy eliminates drift."},{"pattern":"Backends for Frontends (BFF) variant — Consumer Lanes","rationale":"Rather than three separate gateways (expensive to operate), use one gateway with three route groups that apply different filter chains. Mobile lane: JWT + 100rpm. Partner lane: API key + 1000rpm + webhook registration. Internal lane: separate network path entirely."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig (Migration)","rationale":"Migrate services behind the gateway incrementally. Each service is moved by updating DNS/routing. Old direct-access paths are deprecated with a sunset header, then removed."},{"pattern":"Sidecar / Service Mesh for Internal Traffic","rationale":"Internal services need isolation from external traffic, not a heavyweight gateway. A mesh sidecar (Envoy/Linkerd) or simple internal ALB provides mTLS, observability, and circuit breaking without the auth overhead."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox for Webhooks","rationale":"Guarantees at-least-once delivery to partners. Events are written to an outbox table in the same transaction as the domain state change, then delivered asynchronously. Prevents lost webhooks on service crashes."},{"pattern":"Fail-Open Rate Limiting","rationale":"If Redis is unavailable, the gateway allows requests through rather than rejecting all traffic. Counters recover when Redis returns. Protects availability at the cost of temporary rate-limit bypass."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Identity & Access","classification":"generic","aggregates":["UserCredential","PartnerApiKey","Session"]},{"name":"Traffic Policy","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["RateLimitPolicy","ConsumerTier","RouteConfiguration"]},{"name":"Webhook Delivery","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["WebhookSubscription","DeliveryAttempt","OutboxEvent"]},{"name":"Logistics Core","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order","Shipment","Route","Inventory"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Identity & Access","downstream":"API Gateway (Edge)","relationship":"Open Host Service — Auth Service exposes a published validation API consumed by the gateway. Gateway is a conformist to the identity model."},{"upstream":"Traffic Policy","downstream":"API Gateway (Edge)","relationship":"Shared Kernel — rate limit policies and route configs are shared between the gateway config and the policy management system."},{"upstream":"Logistics Core","downstream":"Webhook Delivery","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Core services publish domain events; Webhook Delivery subscribes. Core team (upstream) defines event schemas; Webhook team conforms."},{"upstream":"API Gateway (Edge)","downstream":"Logistics Core","relationship":"Anti-Corruption Layer — Backend services have a thin middleware ACL that translates gateway headers into domain-level caller context, preventing gateway implementation details from leaking into domain code."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single gateway with consumer lanes vs. separate gateways per consumer type","gains":"Lower operational burden — one deployment, one config pipeline, one monitoring stack. Shared infrastructure cost.","sacrifices":"Blast radius is wider — a gateway misconfiguration can affect all consumer types. More complex routing configuration.","alternatives_considered":["Separate gateway per consumer type (higher isolation, 3x operational cost)","No gateway — fix auth consistency at service level (doesn't solve rate limiting centralization, high ongoing maintenance)"]},{"decision":"Fail-open rate limiting","gains":"Availability preserved during Redis outages. Users experience no disruption.","sacrifices":"Temporary loss of rate-limit enforcement during outages. A determined attacker could exploit this window.","alternatives_considered":["Fail-closed (reject all on Redis failure — too disruptive for a logistics platform where uptime is critical)","Local in-memory rate limiting fallback (approximate but better than nothing — good Phase 2 enhancement)"]},{"decision":"Gateway validates auth but services make authorization decisions","gains":"Separation of concerns: gateway handles 'who are you', services handle 'are you allowed to do this'. Services retain control over their authorization rules.","sacrifices":"Authorization logic remains distributed across services (but this is correct — authorization is domain-specific).","alternatives_considered":["Full auth + authz at gateway (couples business rules to infrastructure, creates bottleneck for policy changes)","OPA sidecar for authorization (good for uniform policies, overkill when authz rules are deeply domain-specific)"]},{"decision":"Transactional outbox for webhooks instead of direct dispatch","gains":"At-least-once delivery guarantee. No lost events on crashes. Retry with backoff built in.","sacrifices":"Higher latency for webhook delivery (seconds vs milliseconds). Requires outbox table and poller/CDC process.","alternatives_considered":["Direct HTTP dispatch from service (simpler but unreliable — lost events on failure)","CDC from main database (elegant but complex infrastructure dependency on Debezium/similar)"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Gateway technology choice","evolution_path":"Architecture is gateway-product-agnostic. Can start with Kong/AWS API Gateway/Envoy and swap later. The consumer-lane pattern works on any gateway that supports route-level filter chains."},{"area":"Auth mechanism evolution","evolution_path":"Auth Service is a separate component. Can migrate from API keys to OAuth client credentials for partners, or add OIDC federation, without touching the gateway or backend services."},{"area":"Rate limiting algorithm","evolution_path":"Sliding window, token bucket, or leaky bucket can be swapped by changing the Rate Limiter plugin. Consumer-facing contract (HTTP rate limit headers) stays stable."},{"area":"Webhook delivery mechanism","evolution_path":"Can evolve from simple outbox polling to CDC-based streaming. Can add fan-out, filtering, and transformation without changing the event publishing contract."},{"area":"Internal mesh evolution","evolution_path":"Can start with simple network segmentation + internal ALB, then evolve to full service mesh (Istio/Linkerd) when operational maturity warrants it."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Gateway-injected header contract","reason":"All backend services will depend on X-Consumer-Type, X-Consumer-Id, X-Partner-Id headers. Changing this contract requires coordinated updates across all services. Define it carefully upfront and version it."},{"area":"Rate limit tier definitions","reason":"Partner contracts may encode specific rate limits into SLAs. Changing tier structures has business/legal implications beyond engineering."},{"area":"External API versioning scheme","reason":"Once partners integrate against /v1/*, the versioning strategy (URL path prefix) is locked in. Cannot easily switch to header-based versioning later."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Gateway becomes single point of failure for all external traffic","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy gateway in multi-AZ active-active configuration. Health checks with automatic failover. Canary deployments for config changes. Synthetic monitoring of all three consumer lanes."},{"risk":"Auth Service latency adds to every request","severity":"high","mitigation":"Aggressive caching at gateway: 30s for JWT validation results, 5min for API key resolution. Circuit breaker with cached-result fallback. Auth Service itself should have <5ms p99 (in-memory key validation)."},{"risk":"Migration period: some services behind gateway, some not — inconsistent auth enforcement","severity":"high","mitigation":"Strangler fig migration with strict ordering: highest-risk services first. During migration, old direct paths enforce auth via existing (inconsistent) implementations — no regression. Set hard deadline for full migration."},{"risk":"Redis failure causes temporary rate-limit bypass","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Fail-open with alerting. Add local in-memory approximate rate limiter as fallback (Phase 2). Monitor for abuse patterns during Redis outages."},{"risk":"Webhook delivery backpressure from slow partner endpoints","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Per-partner delivery queues with independent retry. Circuit breaker per partner endpoint. Dead-letter queue after max retries with partner notification."},{"risk":"Internal services accidentally exposed through external gateway","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Network-level isolation: internal services are in a separate VPC/subnet with no route from external gateway. Defense in depth: gateway route config explicitly allowlists only external-facing service paths. Regular audit of exposed routes."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Weeks 1-3): Deploy gateway infrastructure with mobile consumer lane. Migrate 1-2 non-critical services behind gateway. Validate auth offloading and rate limiting. Phase 2 (Weeks 4-6): Add B2B partner lane with API key auth and rate limiting. Migrate remaining external-facing services. Phase 3 (Weeks 7-8): Deploy webhook dispatcher. Migrate partner webhook integrations. Phase 4 (Weeks 9-10): Establish internal gateway/mesh. Remove legacy auth code from backend services. Sunset direct-access paths.","files_to_create_or_modify":["infrastructure/api-gateway/gateway-config.yaml — Gateway route definitions, filter chains per consumer lane","infrastructure/api-gateway/rate-limit-policies.yaml — Per-tier rate limit configurations","services/auth-service/src/ — Auth validation service (if not existing)","services/webhook-dispatcher/src/ — Outbox-based webhook delivery service","shared/middleware/gateway-headers.ts — Backend middleware to parse and validate gateway-injected headers (thin ACL)","infrastructure/terraform/gateway.tf — Infrastructure as code for gateway deployment","docs/adr/001-api-gateway-architecture.md — Architecture decision record"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use declarative gateway configuration (YAML/HCL, not imperative code). Rate limit policies should be data-driven (configurable per consumer tier without code changes). Backend services should have a shared middleware library for gateway header parsing — do not let each team implement their own. Webhook dispatcher should use the transactional outbox pattern with idempotency keys to prevent duplicate delivery.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put business logic in the gateway — it must remain a pure infrastructure concern. The moment domain rules leak into gateway config, you've created an unmaintainable coupling.","Do NOT skip the strangler fig approach and attempt a big-bang migration. That's how you get outages.","Do NOT use the gateway for internal service-to-service calls. Internal traffic must have a separate path — mixing it creates a security boundary violation and a performance bottleneck.","Do NOT implement rate limiting per-service anymore. All rate limiting must go through the gateway to prevent the current inconsistency problem from recurring.","Do NOT hardcode partner rate limits in gateway config. Store them in a database/config service and have the gateway fetch tier definitions. Partners will negotiate different limits."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Three-lane gateway pattern is well-established for multi-consumer platforms","Strangler fig migration is the safe path given existing inconsistent auth","Transactional outbox for webhooks is the industry-standard reliable delivery pattern","Network isolation for internal services is non-negotiable and straightforward"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific gateway product selection (depends on team's existing infrastructure: AWS-native vs Kubernetes-native vs self-managed)","Exact caching TTLs for auth validation (30s/5min are starting points — need load testing to tune)","Whether internal traffic needs a full service mesh or a simpler internal load balancer (depends on scale and operational maturity)"],"assumptions":["The platform runs on cloud infrastructure with VPC/network segmentation capabilities","Redis or equivalent is available or can be provisioned for distributed rate limiting","The team has operational capacity to manage a gateway deployment","Services currently communicate over HTTP/gRPC (not proprietary protocols)","Partner count is in the tens-to-hundreds range (not millions — that would change the API key resolution architecture)"]}}
